name: Rescorla–Wagner Cached-Value Agent with Softmax Action Selection
description: A Pavlovian agent that learns cached state values V(s) for each grid cell via the Rescorla–Wagner (TD(0)) prediction-error rule, then selects actions stochastically via softmax over hunger-modulated Pavlovian action values. The agent does NOT learn action–outcome mappings (no Q(s,a) table); instead, action values are derived from the Pavlovian values of destination states, implementing the 'prepared behavior' constraint (P3). Approach is hardwired toward high-value states, and hunger multiplicatively gates the effective value, capturing state-dependent Pavlovian responding (P4).

variables.name: pavlovian_state_values
variables.description: Dictionary mapping grid cell (x,y) to cached expected reward. Represents the learned CS–US associative strength for each location.

variables.name: reward_prediction_error
variables.description: Signed difference between received reward and predicted value at time t. Positive when reward exceeds expectation, negative otherwise. Encodes the dopaminergic RPE signal.

variables.name: hunger_level
variables.description: Internal deprivation state normalized to [0,1]. Increases each step by alpha_h and decreases by gamma_sat upon eating. Modulates Pavlovian value gain.

variables.name: reward_received
variables.description: Scalar food reward obtained at time t. 0 if no food eaten, r_food if food consumed via eat action.

variables.name: pavlovian_action_values
variables.description: Effective value of each action under Pavlovian control, computed as hunger-modulated V(s_a) minus movement cost. Not stored persistently; recomputed each step.

variables.name: action_probabilities
variables.description: Softmax probability distribution over available actions. Recomputed each step from Q_Pav values.

parameters.name: learning_rate
parameters.source: Empirically validated range 0.01–0.5 for Pavlovian conditioning; Rescorla & Wagner (1972) as cited in Rangel (2013)

parameters.name: inverse_temperature
parameters.source: Standard softmax exploration parameter; validated in prior pipeline runs

parameters.name: hunger_increment_rate
parameters.source: Per-step hunger growth reflecting metabolic demand; calibrated for 10x10 grid

parameters.name: satiation_decrement
parameters.source: Hunger reduction upon eating; calibrated so ~5 meals achieve full satiation from h=1.0

parameters.name: hunger_value_gain
parameters.source: Wanting–hunger coupling; Berridge & Robinson (2003) as cited in Rangel (2013)

parameters.name: movement_cost
parameters.source: Small energetic cost of locomotion to break ties against aimless movement

parameters.name: maximum_value
parameters.source: Upper bound on V(s) for numerical stability; 2x the maximum single-step reward

rules.description: Reward prediction error: the signed difference between received reward and the cached Pavlovian value of the current cell.
Pavlovian value update via Rescorla–Wagner rule: V(s) is incremented proportionally to the prediction error, clipped to [0, R_max].
rules.pseudocode: V[(x, y)] = clip(V.get((x, y), 0.0) + alpha * delta_t, 0.0, R_max)

rules.description: Hunger dynamics: hunger increases by alpha_h each step and decreases by gamma_sat when the agent eats, clipped to [0, 1].
rules.pseudocode:
ate = 1.0 if (action == 'eat' and eat_succeeded) else 0.0
h_t = clip(h_t + alpha_h - gamma_sat * ate, 0.0, 1.0)

rules.description: Hunger-modulated Pavlovian action value: each action's value is the hunger-scaled V of its destination state, minus a small movement cost for movement actions.
Softmax action selection: action probabilities are proportional to exp(beta * Q_Pav(a)), implementing stochastic Pavlovian approach biased toward high-value states.
rules.pseudocode:
max_q = max(Q_Pav.values())  # for numerical stability
for a in available_actions:
  P_a[a] = exp(beta * (Q_Pav[a] - max_q))
Z = sum(P_a.values())
for a in available_actions:
  P_a[a] = P_a[a] / Z
selected_action = random_choice(actions, weights=P_a)

decision_logic.description: Pavlovian agent decision cycle: perceive environment, compute destination-state values for all actions, apply hunger modulation, select action via softmax, then update cached values and hunger after observing outcome. The agent has no model of action-outcome contingencies — it only learns state values V(s) and is hardwired to approach high-V states.
pseudocode: food_positions = set((f['x'], f['y']) for f in food_list)
# 2. COMPUTE DESTINATION CELLS for each action
if 0 <= dx < grid_w and 0 <= dy < grid_h:
exp_vals = {a: exp(beta * (q - max_q)) for a, q in Q_Pav.items()}
P_a = {a: v / Z for a, v in exp_vals.items()}

perception_to_variables.food_at_position: any(f['x'] == perception['x'] and f['y'] == perception['y'] for f in perception['resources']['food'])
perception_to_variables.ate_food: last_action_result — check if eat action succeeded (consumed == true)
perception_to_variables.reward_received: reward argument in update() method; 1.0 from ConsumeEffect on successful eat

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect reward = 1.0; all other actions yield reward = 0.0

expected_behaviors.description: Hunger increases without eating: hunger grows by alpha_h=0.02 per step toward 1.0
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 50 steps without food available → assert h_t > initial_h_t and h_t approx initial_h_t + 50*0.02*(1 accounting for clip)

expected_behaviors.description: Value learning at food locations: after eating at a cell, V(s) increases toward r_food=1.0
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place food at (5,5), agent eats 10 times at (5,5) → assert V[(5,5)] > 0.8

expected_behaviors.description: Approach bias toward high-V cells: agent preferentially moves toward cells with higher learned V values
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set V[(3,3)]=1.0, V[(7,7)]=0.0, agent at (5,5) → over 100 trials, assert more move_left/move_up chosen than move_right/move_down

expected_behaviors.description: Hunger modulates approach: higher hunger increases probability of approach actions to food-rich cells
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set h_t=0.1 vs h_t=0.9, same V map → assert P(approach_food) is higher when h_t=0.9

expected_behaviors.description: Extinction: when food is removed from a previously rewarded cell, V(s) decreases over time
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: train V[(5,5)]=1.0, then agent visits (5,5) 20 times without food → assert V[(5,5)] < 0.5

expected_behaviors.description: Softmax stochasticity: with moderate beta, agent occasionally explores non-optimal directions
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set clear value gradient, run 100 decisions → assert at least some non-greedy actions selected

references: Rangel, A., Camerer, C., & Montague, P. R. (2008). A framework for studying the neurobiology of value-based decision making. Nature Reviews Neuroscience. DOI: 10.1038/nrn2357
Rangel, A. (2013). Regulation of dietary choice by the decision-making circuitry. Nature Neuroscience, 16(12), 1717–1724. DOI: 10.1038/nn.3561
Rescorla, R. A., & Wagner, A. R. (1972). A theory of Pavlovian conditioning: Variations in the effectiveness of reinforcement and nonreinforcement. As cited in Rangel (2013).
